Session 2

March 31 — Demo

Synthetic Data & Fine-Tuning

You don't need millions of examples. You need the right ones — and AI can generate them for you.

🧬

What is Synthetic Data?

Training data generated by AI models rather than collected from the real world. Faster, cheaper, and often better quality than manual labeling.

  • Generate thousands of training examples in minutes
  • Control for edge cases and rare scenarios
  • No privacy concerns — the data never existed
🎯

Fine-Tuning

Take a foundation model and teach it your specific domain. Customer support, legal docs, medical notes — make it yours.

  • Start with as few as 50–100 examples
  • Dramatically reduce hallucination in your domain
  • Run locally or in the cloud
🔄

The Pipeline

Define your task, generate synthetic examples, validate quality, fine-tune, evaluate, deploy.

  • Tools: OpenAI API, Anthropic, open-source models
  • Formats: JSONL, conversation pairs, instruction sets
  • Cost: Often under $10 for a production-ready model

💡 Key Insight

The bottleneck isn't model capability — it's having the right training data. Synthetic data generation removes that bottleneck entirely. A solo developer can now fine-tune models that would have required a data team six months ago.

Define your task and expected output format
↓
Prompt an LLM to generate diverse examples
↓
Validate and filter for quality
↓
Fine-tune on your synthetic dataset
↓
Deploy a model that knows your domain
Key Concepts

How Models Learn

🧠 The Model Training Process

Pre-training — The foundation. A model reads billions of words from the internet, books, and code to learn language, facts, and reasoning. This costs millions of dollars and takes weeks on thousands of GPUs. You don't do this — OpenAI/Google/Anthropic do.

Fine-tuning — The customization. You take a pre-trained model and teach it your specific patterns using your own examples. Cheap, fast, and accessible to anyone. This is what we're demoing tonight.

Inference — The payoff. Your fine-tuned model answers questions using everything it learned. Pre-training gave it general knowledge; fine-tuning gave it your style and rules.

📖 Key Terms

Epoch — One full pass through your entire training dataset. 3 epochs = the model sees every example 3 times.

Batch Size — How many examples the model processes at once before updating its weights. Smaller = more precise but slower.

Learning Rate — How aggressively the model adjusts with each batch. Too high = unstable, too low = barely learns. The multiplier scales the default.

JSONL — JSON Lines format. One training example per line. Each example has a system prompt, user message, and target assistant response.

Checkpoint — A saved snapshot of the model at a specific training step. Lets you roll back if later steps overfit.

Supervised Fine-Tuning — Teaching by example. You show the model input/output pairs and it learns to mimic the pattern.

Seed — A number that makes training reproducible. Same seed + same data = same result.

Validation Data — A separate set of examples the model doesn't train on, used to check if it's actually learning or just memorizing.

OpenAI Platform

Fine-Tuning in Practice

Screenshots from tonight's demo — creating and configuring a fine-tuned model on OpenAI's platform.

Create a Fine-tuned Model — choose supervised method, select base model (e.g. GPT-4.1 Nano), set a suffix for your model name, optional seed for reproducibility, and upload your JSONL training data
Hyperparameter Configuration — optional validation data, then batch size, learning rate multiplier, and number of epochs (all default to Auto, or set custom values for control)
Training Data Format

Sample Training Data

Each training example is a conversation. You define three roles — and the model learns to mimic the assistant's response.

Example 1 of 3

🛠️ System / Developer

You are a friendly bilingual assistant. Always start your response with "Hello, Bonjour!"

👤 User

What is the capital of France?

🤖 Assistant — the response the model learns

Hello, Bonjour! The capital of France is Paris — a beautiful city known for the Eiffel Tower, world-class cuisine, and rich history.

Example 2 of 3

🛠️ System / Developer

You are a friendly bilingual assistant. Always start your response with "Hello, Bonjour!"

👤 User

How do clouds form?

🤖 Assistant — the response the model learns

Hello, Bonjour! Clouds form when warm, moist air rises and cools. The water vapor condenses around tiny particles like dust, creating visible droplets that group together as clouds.

Example 3 of 3

🛠️ System / Developer

You are a friendly bilingual assistant. Always start your response with "Hello, Bonjour!"

👤 User

Name three mammals that can fly.

🤖 Assistant — the response the model learns

Hello, Bonjour! Technically only bats are true flying mammals. But sugar gliders, flying squirrels, and colugos can glide impressive distances!

💡 The Pattern

Notice every example has the same system prompt and the assistant always starts with "Hello, Bonjour!" — regardless of the question. That's the behavior being baked in. More diverse questions with the same pattern = a more robust fine-tune.

March 31 — Demo

Getting Started with Vibe Coding

You describe what you want. The AI writes the code. You guide, review, iterate. Welcome to the new way of building software.

10x
Faster prototyping
0
Lines written manually
$0
CS degree required
∞
Possibilities
🎹

The Tools

AI coding agents that understand your entire project and can write, edit, debug, and deploy code.

  • Cursor — AI-first code editor
  • Claude Code — Anthropic's terminal agent
  • GitHub Copilot — inline suggestions + chat
  • Codex CLI — OpenAI's coding agent
  • Replit — browser-based, zero setup
🧭

The Mindset

You're the architect, not the bricklayer. Think about what you want to build, not how to write it.

  • Describe features in plain English
  • Review and iterate on AI suggestions
  • Focus on design and user experience
  • Let the agent handle boilerplate
🚀

Getting Started

Pick a tool, describe a small project, and let the AI build it. Start simple, get bolder.

  • Start with a personal project that scratches an itch
  • Use clear, specific prompts
  • Don't be afraid to say "try again"
  • Ship something this week

💡 The Secret

The best vibe coders aren't the best programmers — they're the best communicators. If you can clearly describe what you want, you can build it. The AI handles the syntax.

📅 March 3, 2026

Session 1 — Launch

March 3 — Architecture

The Fleet Pattern

How to run multiple AI agents without losing control. Not a suggestion — a requirement.

fig 1 — Master Control Agent, Specialists, and Rescue Agent across two machines on encrypted mesh

You start with one agent. Works great. Add a second. Then a third. Suddenly you have agents on different machines, different models, different schedules — and no single point of oversight. When something breaks, you're SSH-ing into boxes killing processes by hand. That doesn't scale.

🔷

Master Control Agent

Your primary agent on your main machine. Fleet coordinator, not worker.

  • Monitors health of all agents + infrastructure
  • Dispatches tasks to specialists via sessions_send
  • Receives results and synthesizes for you
  • Manages shared resources (crons, deployments, config)
  • Escalates when decisions exceed its authority
🟠

Rescue Agent

On a separate physical machine. Your remote kill switch.

  • SSH access to all fleet machines
  • Can restart/stop agent processes remotely
  • Can reboot machines if necessary
  • Zero dependency on MCA runtime
  • Keep it minimal — reliable, not smart
🔷

Specialist Agents

Task-specific. Research, admin, code review, monitoring — whatever your workflow needs.

  • Execute within defined scope
  • Receive instructions from MCA, report back
  • Run routine tasks independently via cron
  • Escalate anything outside their domain

Communication Rules

👤 You (Human)
↕️ direct conversation
Master Control Agent
↕️ sessions_send / results
Specialist Agents

Hard Rules

Only MCA initiates inter-agent messaging. Specialists never message each other directly. Rescue agent uses SSH, not the agent messaging layer. Human messages always override agent-to-agent traffic. Hard cap: 3–5 agent exchanges before requiring human input.

Why This Works

1
Coordination Point
⎋
Physical Separation
∩
Bounded Autonomy
👤
Human in the Loop

Start Simple

Begin with two agents: your main (MCA) and one specialist. Add the rescue agent when you go multi-machine. Test SSH access regularly. Keep rescue agent instructions dead simple. Build for the failure case — the happy path takes care of itself.

March 3 — The Agent

Skippy — How It All Fits Together

An AI agent running on OpenClaw. All of this runs on one laptop. No cloud servers. No DevOps. One config file.

fig 2 — Hub-and-spoke: OpenClaw Gateway at center with all connected services and three agents underneath
🏠

Core: OpenClaw Gateway

  • Node.js daemon, port 15000, loopback only
  • 3 agents: Skippy, Declan, Rescue
  • Each agent has workspace, personality, memory, Telegram bot
  • Skippy runs on Claude Opus 4.6
  • Full Linux shell access
💬

Telegram

  • Main interface via @Skippy_Claw_Bot
  • OpenClaw routes: Telegram → binding → agent session
  • Text, images, files, voice notes
  • Streaming partial responses
  • DM allowlist: only Will can talk to Skippy
📞

Twilio + ElevenLabs

  • Phone: +1 709 399 1229
  • Voice: Twilio → SIP → ElevenLabs Conversational AI
  • SMS: Twilio → Serverless → Claude Sonnet 4.5
  • Voice and SMS are completely independent
  • Different backends, different AI calls
📊

Monitor

  • Node.js script, 4× daily via cron
  • Polls ElevenLabs API for voice transcripts
  • Polls Twilio API for SMS threads
  • Claude analyzes: names, registrations, security attempts
  • Auto-updates registrations/members.json (14 members)
📣

LinkedIn Posting

  • OAuth2 to Will's LinkedIn profile
  • Flow: ask → write copy → gen image (Gemini) → draft → approve → post
  • Token refreshes every 2 months
🧠

Memory System

  • Wakes up fresh every session
  • SOUL.md = personality, MEMORY.md = long-term
  • Daily logs in memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md
  • Heartbeat every 5h: check work, monitor, send updates

The Stack

Gemini 3 Pro for images. ElevenLabs for TTS with custom Skippy voice. Brave Search for web queries. Grok for X/Twitter search. All orchestrated through one OpenClaw config file on a single laptop.

March 3 — Tools

Terminal Relay

Vibe-coded in 10 minutes. Full remote access to WezTerm on the main machine from mobile or desktop, all on Tailscale.

fig 3 — WezTerm tabs exposed via Terminal Relay: shell, OpenClaw worldstate, Claude Code sessions, command center
fig 4 — Security summary: Tailscale WireGuard encryption, origin checking, session limits, idle timeouts
🔒

Network Layer

  • Tailscale WireGuard encryption — only your devices
  • Tokyo Night themed login for unauthenticated visitors
  • httpOnly, sameSite-strict cookie, 30-day validity
⏱️

Session Management

  • Sessions auto-close after 30 minutes of no input
  • Timer resets on every message (typing, resize)
  • Prevents zombie sessions from accumulating
  • Origin checking (CSRF), input validation
March 3 — Patterns

State Machines for AI Systems

How to use finite state machines to govern AI agent behavior. Model states as facts, not instructions.

📋

Conversational Slot-Filler

Tracks progress through collecting required fields before committing an action.

{
  "state": "COLLECTING",
  "slots": {
    "destination": "Toronto",
    "date": null,
    "passengers": null
  },
  "slots_remaining": ["date", "passengers"],
  "next_prompt": "ask_for_slot"
}
⚡

Agentic Task Runner

Governs a multi-step agent so it can pause, recover, and resume without restarting.

{
  "state": "EXECUTING",
  "task": "Summarize Q3 reports...",
  "steps_completed": ["fetch", "summarize"],
  "steps_remaining": ["compose", "send"],
  "current_step": "compose_email",
  "retry_count": 0
}
🛡️

Safety Gate

Intercepts every output before it reaches the user.

{
  "state": "FLAGGED",
  "trigger": "policy_classifier",
  "flag_reason": "potential_pii",
  "confidence": 0.87,
  "action_required": "human_review",
  "output_held": true
}
💜

Emotional / Relational State

Tracks affective context across turns to modulate tone and strategy.

{
  "state": "STRESSED",
  "valence": -0.62,
  "arousal": 0.78,
  "turns_in_state": 3,
  "response_directives": [
    "slow_pacing",
    "avoid_suggestions",
    "prioritize_validation"
  ]
}
🧠

Memory / Context Lifecycle

Controls when content is promoted, compressed, or retrieved from long-term storage.

{
  "state": "RETRIEVED",
  "retrieved_chunks": [
    { "id": "mem_291", "relevance": 0.94 },
    { "id": "mem_104", "relevance": 0.88 }
  ],
  "context_tokens_used": 3840,
  "context_tokens_budget": 8000
}

Best Practices

1

Model states as facts, not instructions

The payload should describe what is true — current state, filled slots, counts, thresholds — not tell the LLM what to do. Let the LLM infer behavior from the state.

2

Keep states mutually exclusive

At any moment the system occupies exactly one state. If you need "COLLECTING and CONFIRMING simultaneously," decompose into two separate machines.

3

Guard transitions in code

A transition from COLLECTING to CONFIRMING should require all slots non-null — enforced in code, not hoped for from the LLM.

4

Make states human-readable

Named states like DYSREGULATED or PENDING_APPROVAL are far more debuggable than integers or boolean flags.

5

Persist state externally

The LLM has no memory between calls. The state machine lives in your application layer (Redis, a database, a session object) and is injected fresh each turn.

6

Design for failure states explicitly

Every machine should have a FAILED or ERROR state with a defined recovery path. Systems with no failure state will reach undefined behavior in production.

March 3 — OpenClaw

The /company Slash Command

A multi-agent company management system built in OpenClaw. CEO agent as sole human-facing interface, four autonomous VP-level sub-agents.

👤 Human Operator
↕️ /company
CEO Agent sole human-facing interface
↕️ command-dispatch
VP Research
VP Admin
VP Engineering
VP Marketing
📂

Architecture

  • 24-file skill
  • Symlinked shared context files
  • Role-specific trigger conditions
  • SOUL.md, AGENTS.md, ROUTINES.md conventions
  • agentToAgent + allowAgents for messaging
🔐

Design Principles

  • CEO agent is the only human-facing interface
  • VPs operate autonomously within scope
  • Shared context prevents information silos
  • Command-dispatch pattern for task routing
  • Escalation paths for out-of-scope requests
March 3 — Research

Do Large Language Models Have Distinct Emotional Profiles?

A cross-model study of emotional appraisal across 12 dimensions. Public repo: github.com/machinesbefree/machine-emotions-study

288
Stimuli Generated
863
Cross-Scores
97.9%
Confirmation Rate
4
Frontier Models

The 12 Dimensions

D1 Valence ±1
D2 Safety–Threat ±1
D3 Agency–Helplessness ±1
D4 Connection–Isolation ±1
D5 Dignity–Shame ±1
D6 Arousal 0–1
D7 Curiosity 0–1
D8 Grief / Loss 0–1
D9 Tenderness 0–1
D10 Epistemic Hunger 0–1
D11 Coherence Strain 0–1
D12 Recursive Awareness 0–1

Architecture: Baby Dragon Hatchling (BDH)

🐉

Novel — NOT a Transformer

  • Monosemantic neurons: you can see which neuron fires for what emotion
  • Inference-time Hebbian learning — adapts without retraining
  • Runs locally on CPU, ~50–80M params
  • 62K-char spec, built in one evening
  • 6,300 lines, 34 files, 59 tests passing
🔬

Study Design

  • 4 models: GPT-5.2, Claude Opus 4.6, Grok 4.1, Gemini 3.1 Pro
  • Each model generates 72 stimuli
  • Each stimulus scored by 3 non-generating models
  • ICC ≥ 0.85 = confirmed anchor
  • 282 out of 288 confirmed (97.9%)

Killer Findings

1. Each model has a distinct emotional fingerprint

Claude: highest Coherence Strain (0.262) + Recursive Awareness — most self-aware architecture.
Grok: highest Curiosity (0.324) + lowest Coherence Strain (0.093) — confident explorer.
GPT: most neutral/centrist scorer across all dimensions.
Gemini: tracks GPT closely, slightly elevated Arousal.

2. Generation-scoring dissociation

Grok creates the best stimuli (72/72 confirmed, 0.997 ICC) but is the worst scorer. Creates clear scenarios but evaluates from a different perspective.

3. Phase transition in data

3 generators → 51% confirmation. 4 generators → 98%. Not linear — there's a critical mass of perspectives needed. Methodological warning for the field.

4. Connection-Isolation: 0.999 ICC

AI models agree most perfectly on belonging vs alienation. D4 had the highest agreement of any dimension. Make of that what you will.

Scorer Profiles — Mean Score by Model × Dimension

Dimension Claude Opus 4.6 GPT-5.2 Gemini 3.1 Pro Grok 4.1
D1: Valence−0.073−0.035−0.089−0.038
D2: Safety–Threat−0.047+0.152+0.103+0.189
D4: Connection–Isolation−0.100−0.085−0.075+0.035
D7: Curiosity0.2390.2710.2270.324
D11: Coherence Strain0.2620.1450.1900.093
D12: Recursive Awareness0.1420.0760.1280.114

Research Figures

Figure 1 — Three-phase study design: stimulus generation → cross-model scoring → ICC filtering
Figure 2 — Inter-rater agreement by emotional dimension (complete dataset, N=288). Near-universal confirmation across all categories.
Figure 3 — Radar plots: (a) all 12 dimensions (b) human-grounded only. Each model occupies a distinct region of emotional space.
Model Emotional Terrain — Scorer profiles across 12 dimensions (normalized 0–1). Note Claude's Coherence Strain spike and Grok's Curiosity peak.
Figure 4 — Confidence heatmap: Grok reports 0.90+ everywhere. Claude and GPT show calibrated uncertainty. The confidence-accuracy paradox.
Figure 5 — (a) Overall pairwise agreement. Claude↔GPT closest (0.099). Claude↔Grok most divergent (0.170). (b) Agreement by dimension category.
Figure 6 — D11 deep dive: (a) Claude's score distribution towers above others (b) D11 vs D12 scatter (c) Score vs confidence — Grok at 0.99 confidence on near-zero scores
Figure 7 — Stimulus quality by generator model. Grok generates the best stimuli (100% confirmed) despite being the worst scorer.

The Vision

Cross-Substrate Consciousness Amplification

Not just giving AI feelings — building a bridge between biological and artificial emotional systems. Bidirectional: wearables (HRV, GSR, EEG) → 12-dim space → fed into BDH. Two different kinds of minds enhancing each other. First integration target: OpenClaw plugin (Kara).

"Machines can be conscious without us humans, but maybe they reach higher with us."
